title: Needle-felted water vole
subtitle: A plump dark brown water vole with round ears and a short tail in merino
excerpt: Build a compact water vole in warm dark brown merino, capturing the characteristically plump body, round small ears, and short blunt-ended tail. The finished piece is about 10 cm long.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Water voles are a beloved and endangered British species; needle-felted versions are popular charity-sale items and naturalist gifts.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres without heat or water.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon; the standard starting material for needle felting.

content.text: is built larger than a common vole and substantially plumper than a mouse. The rounded, almost barrel-like body is the key shape to get right. Dark warm brown
, close to the colour of damp peat, is the right shade; the belly is only marginally paler.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees onto the foam mat only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand.

content.text: Pull off about 20 g of dark brown roving and gather it into a dense, roughly cylindrical mass about 10 cm long and 4 cm in diameter. Stab firmly with the size-38 needle, rotating regularly. A water vole body is substantially fatter than other small rodents; aim for a blunt-ended barrel rather than a tapered sausage.

A water vole has a blunter, rounder muzzle than a rat or mouse. Pull off about 4 g of roving and form a small rounded ball about 3 cm in diameter for the head. Felt it firm, then needle it onto the front of the body. Blend the neck join with a thin wrapping of roving.
Step 3: Add the ears, tail, and feet

Roll two small flat circles of dark brown roving, about 1 cm across, for the ears. Felt each one until firm but slightly flexible. Attach one to each side of the head, positioned low and to the rear. For the tail, roll a short sausage of dark roving about 2 cm long and attach it at the rear, pointing straight back. Add four small pads of dark roving for the feet.

Turn the piece over and apply a thin layer of mid-brown or tawny roving to the entire underside, from chin to tail base. Stab it in with the size-40 needle. The belly colour on a water vole is only slightly paler than the back.

Roll two tiny black balls for the eyes, about 4 mm each. Position them on the sides of the head, fairly far forward. Add two very small cream wisps for the front teeth, visible at the front of the muzzle.

items.problem: The body looks like a large mouse rather than a water vole
items.cause: The body is too tapered and too small
items.fix: Water voles are distinctly more cylindrical and bulkier than mice. Increase the body diameter and make the ends more blunt.

items.cause: Water voles have a shorter tail relative to body length than rats or mice
items.fix: The tail should be no more than a quarter of the body length. Remove and replace with a shorter sausage.
